request.question: "The Stumble" is a blues guitar instrumental composed by a guitarist born in which year ?

title: Earl Hooker
Freddie King
Jarekus Singleton
Guitar Boogie (song)
Hide Away
Cornell Dupree
The Stumble
Selwyn Birchwood
Chitlins con Carne
Music of North Carolina
sentences: Earl Hooker (January 15, 1930 – April 21, 1970) was a Chicago blues guitarist known for his slide guitar playing.
Considered a "musician's musician", he performed with blues artists such as Sonny Boy Williamson II, Junior Wells, and John Lee Hooker and fronted his own bands.
An early player of the electric guitar, Hooker was influenced by the modern urban styles of T-Bone Walker and Robert Nighthawk.
He recorded several singles and albums as a bandleader and with other well-known artists.
His "Blue Guitar", a slide guitar instrumental single, was popular in the Chicago area and was later overdubbed with vocals by Muddy Waters as "You Shook Me".
Freddie King (September 3, 1934 – December 28, 1976) was an American blues guitarist and singer.
He has been described as one of the "Three Kings" of electric blues guitar, along with Albert King and B.B. King.
He was an influential guitarist with hits for Federal Records in the early 1960s.
His soulful and powerful voice and distinctive guitar style inspired countless musicians, particularly guitarists (Eric Clapton is a notable example).
He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2012.
Jarekus Singleton (born July 11, 1984 in Clinton, Mississippi, United States) is an American blues guitarist, singer and songwriter.
"Refuse To Lose" was his first nationally and internationally distributed album.
It was released on the Chicago-based independent blues record label Alligator Records on May 6, 2014.
England's "Blues & Rhythm" called Singleton "a great, new blues talent…young, original, soulful and intense…superb, blistering guitar."

According to "Living Blues" magazine, "Jarekus Singleton is making some serious blues noise...blending modern-day blues and emotionally intense soul with melodic, hot-toned lead guitar, funk-seasoned rhythms and hip-hop flavored lyrics."
"The Washington Post" said, "Jarekus Singleton is an exciting new young blues guitarist with melody, hooks, swagger and a strong, original voice.
His lyrics are modern, personal, acutely poetic and deeply mature."
"USA Today" said of Singleton, "Stinging blues guitar and potent, original songs herald the emergence of a major new talent."
"Guitar Boogie" is a guitar instrumental recorded by Arthur "Guitar Boogie" Smith in 1945.
It was one of the first recordings in the style later dubbed "hillbilly boogie" to reach a widespread audience, and eventually sold nearly three million copies.
It was the first guitar instrumental to climb the country music charts, and then crossover and also gain high rankings on the popular music charts.
"Guitar Boogie" has been interpreted and recorded by a variety of musicians.
It is among the songs discussed as the first rock and roll record.
"Hide Away" or "Hideaway" is a blues guitar instrumental that has become "a standard for countless blues and rock musicians performing today".
First recorded in 1960 by Freddie King, the song became an R&B and pop chart hit.
Since then, it has been interpreted and recorded by numerous blues and other musicians and has been recognized by the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and the Grammy Hall of Fame.
Cornell Luther Dupree (December 19, 1942 – May 8, 2011) was an American jazz and R&B guitarist.

He worked at various times with Aretha Franklin, Bill Withers, Donny Hathaway, King Curtis and Steve Gadd, appeared on David Letterman, and wrote a book on soul and blues guitar: "Rhythm and Blues Guitar."
He reportedly recorded on 2,500 sessions.
"The Stumble" is a blues guitar instrumental composed and recorded by American blues artist Freddie King, for his 1961 album "Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King", (King 773).
It was released as the fourth single from this album in 1962.
It is considered a blues classic and follows in a string of popular instrumentals recorded by King in the early 1960s, including "Hide Away", "San-Ho-Zay", and "Sen-Sa-Shun".
Selwyn Birchwood (born March 9, 1985) is an American blues guitarist, vocalist and songwriter from Tampa, Florida.
He was the winner of the Blues Foundation’s 2013 International Blues Challenge (band category), as well the winner of the Albert King Guitarist of the Year award, presented at the same event.
To win, he bested 125 other bands from around the world.
Birchwood plays electric guitar and electric lap steel guitar.
His live performances feature his original songs. "
Living Blues" magazine said, "Selwyn Birchwood is making waves, surprising people and defying expectations.
Be on the lookout.
He revels in the unexpected." "
The Tampa Tribune" said Birchwood plays with "power and precision reminiscent of blues guitar hero Buddy Guy.
He is a gritty vocalist [who is] commanding with his axe."
"Rolling Stone" said "Birchwood is a young, powerhouse guitarist and soulful vocalist.
"Don’t Call No Ambulance" is a remarkable debut by a major player."

"The Washington Post" said, "Selwyn Birchwood is an indelibly modern and original next-generation bluesman; his tough vocals, guitar and lap steel touch on classic Chicago blues, Southern soul and boogie."
"Chitlins con Carne" is a jazz blues instrumental composed by guitarist Kenny Burrell and first released on his 1963 album "Midnight Blue".
The original version featured Burrell on guitar, Stanley Turrentine on tenor saxophone, Major Holley on bass, Billy Gene English on drums, and Ray Barretto on congas.
North Carolina is known particularly for its tradition of old-time music, and many recordings were made in the early 20th century by folk song collector Bascom Lamar Lunsford.

Most influentially, North Carolina country musicians like the North Carolina Ramblers and Al Hopkins helped solidify the sound of country music in the late 1920s, while influential bluegrass musicians such as Earl Scruggs, Doc Watson and Del McCoury came from North Carolina.
Arthur Smith is the most notable North Carolina musician/entertainer who had the first nationally syndicated television program which featured country music.
Smith composed "Guitar Boogie", the all-time best selling guitar instrumental, and "Dueling Banjos", the all-time best selling banjo composition.
Country rock star Eric Church from the Hickory area has had 2 #1 albums on the Billboard 200, including "Chief" in 2011.
Both North and South Carolina are a hotbed for traditional country blues, especially the style known as the Piedmont blues.
Elizabeth Cotten, from Chapel Hill, was active in the American folk music revival.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: "The Stumble" is a blues guitar instrumental composed by a guitarist born in which year ?
hop1_docs: The Stumble | "The Stumble" is a blues guitar instrumental composed and recorded by American blues artist Freddie King, for his 1961 album "Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King", (King 773).  It was released as the fourth single from this album in 1962.  It is considered a blues classic and follows in a string of popular instrumentals recorded by King in the early 1960s, including "Hide Away", "San-Ho-Zay", and "Sen-Sa-Shun".

Classical guitar repertoire | To a greater extent than most other instruments and ensembles, it is difficult to compose music for the guitar without either proficiency in the instrument or close collaboration with a guitarist.  As a result, a large part of the guitar repertoire consists of works by guitarists who did not compose extensively for other instruments.  Music prior to the classical era was often composed for performance on various combinations of instruments, and could be adapted by the performer to keyboard instruments, the lute, or the guitar.  Since the beginning of the 20th century, however, a significant amount of music has been written for the guitar by non-guitarist composers.

Blues Saraceno | Blues Saraceno (born October 17, 1971) is an American rock guitarist, composer and music producer, currently residing in Los Angeles, California.  He was discovered by "Guitar for the Practicing Musician" magazine at the age of 16, which assisted him in releasing instrumental recordings on an independent basis.  Saraceno's high profile as a gifted guitar virtuoso and musician opened the doors to an early career as a first-call guitar sideman and session musician.  Saraceno is most often recognized from his time playing with Jack Bruce and Ginger Baker (Cream) as well as his brief tenure with the Hard rock band Poison.  Saraceno's early success in the music industry would eventually blossom into a career in producing and composing for television and film.

Mike Bloomfield | Michael Bernard Bloomfield (July 28, 1943 – February 15, 1981) was an American guitarist and composer, born in Chicago, Illinois, who became one of the first popular music superstars of the 1960s to earn his reputation almost entirely on his instrumental prowess, since he rarely sang before 1969.  Respected for his guitar playing, Bloomfield knew and played with many of Chicago's blues legends before achieving his own fame and was instrumental in popularizing blues music in the mid-1960s.  He was ranked number 22 on Rolling Stone's list of "100 Greatest Guitarists of All Time" in 2003 and number 42 by the same magazine in 2011.  He was inducted into the Blues Hall of Fame in 2012 and, as a member of the Paul Butterfield Blues Band, was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2015.

Chitlins con Carne | "Chitlins con Carne" is a jazz blues instrumental composed by guitarist Kenny Burrell and first released on his 1963 album "Midnight Blue".  The original version featured Burrell on guitar, Stanley Turrentine on tenor saxophone, Major Holley on bass, Billy Gene English on drums, and Ray Barretto on congas.

Steelism | Steelism are a Nashville-based instrumental band.  A "country twang, jazz, blues, R&B, surf guitar, and Ennio Morricone-like movie soundtrack washed in a 21st century blend," Steelism is composed of guitarist Jeremy Fetzer and pedal steel player Spencer Cullum.

List of bass guitarists | This is a list of electric bass guitar players that have their own separate article in Wikipedia.  The bass guitar is a stringed instrument played primarily with the fingers (either by plucking, slapping, popping, or tapping) or using a pick.  Since the 1950s, the electric bass guitar has largely replaced the double bass in popular music.  Bass guitarists provide the low-pitched basslines and bass runs in many different styles of music ranging from rock and metal to blues and jazz.  Bassists also use the bass guitar as a soloing instrument in jazz, fusion, Latin, funk, and in some rock styles.  Musicians known mainly as guitarists are listed separately in the list of guitarists.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Freddie King — passage [1] "The Stumble | '...composed and recorded by American blues artist Freddie King'" (mentions composer).
- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing birth year: none of the supplied passages state Freddie King's year of birth.
  - Minor spelling variant in album title ("Freddy King") may appear, but the composer is identified as Freddie King in the text.
- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Need Freddie King's year of birth (birth date/year) to answer the question.
- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Wikipedia page: "Freddie King"
  2. Search query: "Freddie King birth year" or "Freddie King born"
  3. Reliable biographies or music databases: "Freddie King biography" (AllMusic, Oxford Music Online, or Blues Hall of Fame entry)
hop2_docs: Freddy King Sings | Freddy King Sings is an album by blues singer and guitarist Freddie King.  Released in 1961, it was King's first album and includes four singles that appeared in "Billboard" magazine's R&B and Pop charts.  In 2008, "Freddy King Sings" was inducted into the Blues Foundation Hall of Fame in the "Classics of Blues Recordings" category.

Little Freddie King | Little Freddie King (born Fread Eugene Martin, July 19, 1940) is an American Delta blues guitarist.  His style is based on that of Freddie King, but his approach to country blues is original.

Freddy Cole | Lionel Frederick "Freddy" Cole (born October 15, 1931) is an American jazz singer and pianist whose recording career has spanned over sixty years.  He is the brother of musicians Nat King Cole and Ike Cole, father of Lionel Cole, and uncle of Natalie Cole, Timolin Cole, and Casey Cole.

Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King | Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King is a 1961 instrumental album by blues guitarist and singer Freddie "The Texas Cannonball" King.  Released on King Records, the album contained a number of influential songs and two hit singles, "Hide Away" and "San-Ho-Zay".  The former reached #5 on the "Black Singles" chart and #29 on the "Pop Singles" chart, while the latter reached #4 and #47.  The album, itself influential, has been critically well received.

Benny Turner | Benny Turner is an American blues musician.  He is the younger brother of Freddie King and was the bass guitarist for the Freddie King Band.  Later, Turner joined Mighty Joe Young as the bass guitarist of his band before becoming the bandleader for Marva Wright for 20 years.  Turner received the Independent Music Award for best blues song for "I Can't Leave" and his album "When She's Gone" was nominated for best traditional blues CD and best Soul Blues Album at the Blues Blast Awards in 2016.

Freddie Miller (rugby league) | Freddie Miller (birth unknown - death unknown) was a professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1930s, 1940s, and 1950s, playing at club level for Hull F.C., and Featherstone Rovers (Heritage № 314), as a fullback , i.e. number 1, and was Captain of Hull during the 1946–47 season.

The Stumble | "The Stumble" is a blues guitar instrumental composed and recorded by American blues artist Freddie King, for his 1961 album "Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King", (King 773).  It was released as the fourth single from this album in 1962.  It is considered a blues classic and follows in a string of popular instrumentals recorded by King in the early 1960s, including "Hide Away", "San-Ho-Zay", and "Sen-Sa-Shun".
hop2_titles: Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King
response.summary_2: Freddie King — composer of "The Stumble" — is identified in the provided passages, but none of the supplied passages state his year of birth, so the birth year is not available from these sources.
response.answer: Not available from the provided summaries.
